steps.title: ðŸ¤– How does ChatGPT work?
steps.content:
ChatGPT is an artificial intelligence program trained to understand and generate text.

It was fed with billions of examples from books, websites, articles, and conversations. Through this, it learned patterns of human language.

When you ask a question, it analyzes the context and predicts, word by word, the best possible answer based on these patterns.

It doesn't "know" things like a human does. It recognizes patterns and responds based on probability.

The better your prompt, the better the result will be.

It is one of the most used artificial intelligences in the world. It surpassed 100 million users in just 2 months after its launch.

Today it is used by students, businesses, programmers, content creators, and large corporations.

It is integrated into tools such as browsers, applications, enterprise systems, and productivity platforms.

Companies use AI to reduce task time by up to 40% in administrative and text production activities.

steps.title: ðŸ‘ Where ChatGPT is very good
steps.content:
ChatGPT stands out when the work involves:

- Creating texts, scripts, emails, ads, and content
- Organizing loose ideas into something logical and strategic
- Helping with studies, summaries, and explanations
- Planning marketing, sales, or content strategies
- Analyzing textual information and "connecting the dots" between data
- Saving time on repetitive or creative tasks

steps.title: ðŸ‘Ž Where ChatGPT is very bad (and shouldn't be used)
steps.content:
ChatGPT <bold> is not recommended </bold> for:

- Diagnosing diseases or interpreting medical exams
- Predicting stock prices, cryptocurrencies, or future results with certainty
- Executing physical actions or making critical decisions alone
- Accessing real-time information without extra tools
- Replacing specialists in sensitive areas

steps.title: âš ï¸ Attention is not consciousness
steps.content:
AI uses a 'mathematical flashlight' to focus on what is relevant in your sentence.

If you ask: 'Who wrote Dom Casmurro?', the flashlight focuses on 'wrote' and 'Dom Casmurro'. It ignores the rest to search for the most probable connection: Machado de Assis.

This allows it to maintain meaning in long conversations, but remember: it's just a relevance calculation, not a real interest in the subject.

steps.title: âŒ Hallucination: When AI 'Makes Things Up'
steps.content:
The 'Hallucination' is not a system error, it's how it works.

The AI's goal is to be convincing, not necessarily truthful. If you ask it to cite a study that doesn't exist, it might invent a title and authors just to deliver what you asked for.

Treat AI as an initial draft, never as the final word.

steps.title: âš™ï¸ Personalization: making ChatGPT respond your way
steps.content:
ChatGPT doesn't have just one way of responding. It adapts to the context you provide.

Go to:
Settings â†’ Personalization

There is no ideal text. The best text is the one that matches your goal and your way of learning or working.

You can adjust:
â€“ the tone (simple, direct, technical)
â€“ the type of explanation (short or detailed)
â€“ the main use (study, work, content creation)

Start simple. Over time, adjust as you feel the need.

steps.title: ðŸ’¡ Example Prompt
steps.content:
You can use something like this and adapt it to your style:

'I want clear, practical, and objective answers. I prefer simple language and examples when possible. I use ChatGPT to learn, organize ideas, and create content. Avoid generic responses.'

This text already greatly improves the quality of the answers â€” and can be adjusted to your style whenever you want.

steps.title: ðŸ§  Tokens: The Short 'Memory' of AI
steps.content:
AI doesn't read words like humans. It processes tokens - small fragments of text that can be parts of words, whole words, or even punctuation marks.

Each model has a maximum limit of tokens it can analyze at the same time. This limit is called the context window. Inside this window go: your question, the previous messages of the conversation, and the response the AI will generate itself.

When the total number of tokens exceeds this limit, the oldest parts of the conversation are no longer considered. It's not that the AI "forgets" like a human â€” it's a technical limitation of the system.

Therefore, being clear, objective, and strategic when writing your prompts helps preserve relevant context and increases the quality of the answers.

steps.title: âš ï¸ Lesson: Speaking well is not the same as being right
steps.content:
ChatGPT writes very well. This is one of the tool's greatest strengths â€” and also one of its biggest risks.

It masters the form of the text: structure, language, fluidity, and a convincing tone. But this doesn't mean that everything it writes is true.

ChatGPT doesn't verify facts like a human would. It doesn't check sources in real-time or confirm if a study really exists. It generates answers based on language patterns that seem correct.

Because of this, it can create:
â€“ well-written articles with inaccurate data
â€“ convincing, yet incomplete, explanations
â€“ citations that sound real, but don't exist

The text might be elegant. That doesn't guarantee it's true.

steps.title: ðŸŽ¯ Prompt Engineering
steps.content:
It's learning to give clear commands to the AI. The better the instruction, the better the answer.

A prompt directs the model's response by providing specific context and instructions. A prompt is the command you give to the artificial intelligence.

You can ask it to act like an expert on a subject before asking what you want. Everything ChatGPT answers starts from the prompt.

Example:
â€œWrite a story about a cat.â€
This is already a prompt. The AI uses this instruction as a baseline to generate the response â€” in this case, a story about a cat.
The simpler the prompt, the simpler the response tends to be. The clearer and more targeted, the more useful and personalized it becomes. You can also send images to complement the prompt.

steps.title: ðŸ“Š How to Know if the Answer is Good?
steps.content:
Always ask these 3 questions after receiving a response from the AI:

1. **Is it complete?** Did it answer everything I asked?
2. **Is it true?** Do the cited facts and names really exist?
3. **Is it useful?** Does this solve my problem or is it just 'fluff'?

If it's not perfect, don't give up: ask it to adjust the missing points.

props.explanation: Never accept the first answer if it doesn't pass this quality filter!

steps.title: ðŸŽ¨ How image creation works
steps.content:
When you ask for an image, the AI interprets your text and transforms it into visual elements.

It analyzes:
â€“ characters
â€“ setting
â€“ visual style
â€“ colors
â€“ mood/emotion
â€“ framing

If something is not stated, the AI assumes it. And that's when generic or unexpected images appear.

steps.title: ðŸ“ Simple structure to create good images
steps.content:
Always use this reasoning to avoid mistakes:

**What is the image?**
Person, object, scene, illustration, photo, digital art.

**Where does it happen?**
Environment, background, location, context.

**Visual style**
Realistic, cinematic, illustration, cartoon, 3D, minimalist, artistic.

**Mood or emotion**
Serious, inspiring, dramatic, joyful, mysterious.

**Important details**
Colors, clothes, expression, light, camera angle.

steps.title: ðŸ’¡ Image prompt examples
steps.content:
Create an image of a young man using a laptop in a modern environment, with natural light, realistic style, professional mood, and neutral colors. (The clearer, the better the result).

**Advanced Example:**
An anthropomorphic banana in cartoon style, with arms and legs, relaxing on a beach chair. The banana is smiling, wearing black sunglasses, with a relaxed and fun expression.

â€“ **Setting:** sunny tropical beach, light sand, turquoise blue sea, clear sky with few clouds, and an island in the background.
â€“ **Visual style:** high-quality digital illustration, vibrant colors, soft lighting, cheerful and relaxed mood.
â€“ **Composition:** the banana is the main focus of the image, in the foreground, without a cellphone or other technological objects.
â€“ **Aesthetics:** clean, fun, and modern, ideal for creative and educational content.
â€“ **Style:** cartoon / 3D illustration / Pixar-like
â€“ **Quality:** high detail, ultra quality
â€“ **Format:** vertical (9:16) if using for stories or Reels

steps.title: ðŸŽ™ï¸ ChatGPT Voice Chat
steps.content:
Voice chat allows you to speak to ChatGPT instead of typing. You ask the question speaking normally and receive the answer via audio.

It is useful for:
â€“ quick questions
â€“ organizing ideas
â€“ thinking out loud
â€“ using when you can't type

You don't need to speak technically. You can converse naturally and adjust the answer during the dialogue. Just like with text, the answer can be wrong. Use voice chat for clarity and agility, not as the final source of truth.

steps.title: ðŸ”Ž ChatGPT as an investigation tool
steps.content:
ChatGPT isn't just for generating texts. It can be used as a strategic investigation tool.

When properly directed, it helps organize complex problems, raise hypotheses, compare scenarios, and identify hidden risks. Instead of just delivering an answer, it structures reasoning.

The secret lies in how you ask.

steps.title: ðŸŽ¯ Practical investigation example
steps.content:
**Common Question (Bad):**
â€œIs it worth opening a marketing agency?â€

**Investigative Question (Excellent):**
â€œAnalyze the feasibility of opening a digital marketing agency in Brazil considering: level of competition, barriers to entry, differentiation, dependence on paid traffic, revenue predictability, and main risks in the first 12 months.â€

**What does it do in this case?**
It organizes the analysis by criteria, points out opportunities, risks, possible financial bottlenecks, and helps you see the scenario more clearly.

**But beware:** it structures thought â€” it does not guarantee the truth of the data. Investigation with AI is intelligence + human validation.

options.text: It helps organize complex problems, raise hypotheses, and identify risks.
options.explanation: Correct! This is exactly how to use AI as a strategic tool.

options.text: It can only investigate if you ask short and simple questions.
options.explanation: Incorrect. Detailed questions generate much better investigations.

options.text: Voice Chat is not used for investigation, only text.
options.explanation: Incorrect. You can use voice chat to think out loud and organize ideas.

steps.title: ðŸš€ Extracting the Maximum from ChatGPT
steps.content:
ChatGPT delivers performance when you think before asking. Don't ask for â€œjust anythingâ€. Define the scenario, objective, and expected outcome.

Always include enough context for it to understand the problem and tell it exactly how you want to receive the answer (bullet points, table, executive summary, step-by-step). The more directed the command, the less generic the answer will be.

**Practical tip:** Before sending the prompt, ask yourself â€” â€œIf I were an employee, would I understand exactly what I need to deliver?â€

steps.title: âœï¸ Writing with Clarity and Impact
steps.content:
ChatGPT is extremely strong at structuring texts, improving arguments, and adjusting tone. To extract the best, specify: target audience, communication goal, and desired style.

If you want more impact, ask for alternative versions. If you want more clarity, ask for simplification while keeping the core idea.

**Practical tip:** Use commands like â€œreorganize to make it more persuasiveâ€, â€œreduce by 30% without losing impactâ€, or â€œmake it more direct and less genericâ€.

steps.title: ðŸ“Š Strategic Planning
steps.content:
It is excellent for turning vague goals into structured plans. Always ask for a breakdown by stages, priority, and deadline.

If the planning involves financial risk or positioning, also ask for an analysis of bottlenecks and potential failures.

**Practical tip:** End the request with â€œinclude the main risks and how to mitigate themâ€. This elevates the level of the response.

steps.title: ðŸ”Ž Scenario Analysis and Comparison
steps.content:
ChatGPT organizes reasoning very efficiently when you provide criteria. Don't just ask for an opinion.

Ask for a comparison based on clear factors like cost, scalability, risk, and return. If you want depth, ask for counterarguments and blind spots.

**Practical tip:** Use questions like â€œwhat is the worst-case scenario?â€ and â€œwhat premises could be wrong in this analysis?â€.

steps.title: ðŸ“ˆ Structuring Spreadsheets
steps.content:
It is strong at organizing data in a logical format. To extract the most, explain the purpose of the spreadsheet: control, analysis, or projection.

Ask for suggestions on strategic columns and relevant performance indicators.

**Practical tip:** Always ask to â€œorganize in table format with clear columns and recommended indicatorsâ€.

steps.title: ðŸ“š Smart Summaries
steps.content:
ChatGPT is very efficient at condensing information, but you need to state the focus of the summary.

Do you want a focus on decision-making? A focus on learning? Just the main arguments? Specify that.

**Practical tip:** Use commands like â€œsummarize into 5 actionable pointsâ€ or â€œturn into an executive summary for decision-makingâ€.

steps.title: ðŸŽ¯ The Golden Rule
steps.content:
The more context, clear objective, and defined format you provide, the better the result will be.

ChatGPT doesn't read your intention. It responds to what you specify. Those who learn to ask with precision extract above-average answers.

steps.title: ðŸ› ï¸ Exercise 5: Improving Weak Prompts
steps.content:
Transforming bad requests into strategic ones makes all the difference.

**âœ… Strategic Prompt:**
â€œCreate a monthly sales control spreadsheet in table format, including columns for date, product, amount, sales channel, acquisition cost, and net profit. Also suggest important indicators for performance analysis.â€

This is the practical application of the golden rule: **Context + Objective + Defined Format**.

steps.title: ðŸ• The History of Claude
steps.content:
Claude is an artificial intelligence developed by Anthropic, an AI safety-focused company. It was born in response to the need to create a safer assistant, aligned with human values and less prone to generating harmful content. Unlike other AI available on the market, Claude was built with an approach centered on reliability and transparency. Anthropic invested heavily in training techniques that allow Claude to be honest about its limitations, admit when it doesn't know something, and refuse harmful tasks respectfully.

Practical tip: when using Claude, you are interacting with an AI that was deliberately trained to be more cautious and honest â€” this means sometimes it will clearly say "I cannot do this", which is actually a sign of quality.

steps.title: âœ… Where Claude is Very Good
steps.content:
Claude excels in professional writing, deep text analysis, structuring complex ideas, and creating creative content that requires nuance. It is excellent for reviewing and improving arguments, structuring projects, analyzing long documents, generating well-explained code, and thinking through ethical or nuanced problems. If you need an assistant that asks clarifying questions before answering, considers multiple perspectives, and delivers well-founded answers, Claude is your ideal tool.

Practical tip: use Claude when you need deep analysis â€” it is particularly effective at reading entire documents, identifying patterns, and synthesizing complex insights clearly.

steps.title: âŒ When Not to Use Claude
steps.content:
Claude is not the best choice for tasks that require real-time information â€” such as current stock quotes, today's news, or the current status of public figures. It also does not replace specialist academic research and should not be your sole resource for critical health, legal, or financial decisions. When you need ultra-fast and superficial answers without depth, other tools might be more efficient. Claude also takes deliberate care with certain types of content for ethical reasons, meaning it might refuse some requests that other AIs would accept.

Practical tip: if your question is about "what happened yesterday in politics?" or "what is the price of Bitcoin right now?", use web search or specialized tools â€” don't rely solely on Claude for these data.

steps.title: ðŸ¤– Claude's Models
steps.content:
Claude is available in three main versions: Claude Opus, the most powerful and capable model; Claude Sonnet, the balanced model that offers good performance with speed; and Claude Haiku, the fastest and lightest model for simple queries. Each version was optimized for different needs â€” the more sophisticated your problem, the more robust the model you should use. Anthropic regularly updates these versions, so there is always a more recent iteration available.

Practical tip: start with Claude Sonnet for most tasks â€” it offers the best balance between quality and speed without being overkill for simple queries.

steps.title: ðŸ’¬ How Claude Likes Its Users to Send Prompts
steps.content:
Claude works best when you are clear, specific, and direct. You don't need to be overly polite â€” be natural. Explain the context and the goal: "I want an email for a dissatisfied client" is better than just "write an email". If you want a specific style, mention it: "professional but friendly tone" or "as if it were a conversation between friends". Claude appreciates it when you ask for iterations and refinements â€” tell it exactly what you didn't like so it can adjust. Asking clarifying questions is encouraged, so if Claude asks for additional context, answer in detail.

Practical tip: before sending your prompt, ask yourself: "If I were to explain this to a coworker, what would I say?" â€” that is the clarity Claude appreciates.

steps.title: âš–ï¸ Claude vs ChatGPT
steps.content:
While ChatGPT is more popular and has real-time web integration, Claude tends to provide more structured and reflective answers. ChatGPT is faster at generating generic content, but Claude is more careful when reviewing arguments and more prone to questioning premises before responding. In terms of pure creativity, both are capable, but Claude frequently offers more nuanced perspectives. ChatGPT is better for finding recent information, while Claude is superior in deep analysis, reviewing complex texts, and tasks requiring ethical reasoning. The real choice is: do you prefer speed or depth? Do you prefer direct answers or do you want the AI to question your query first?

Practical tip: use ChatGPT to "generate 10 quick ideas" and Claude to "help me refine this complex strategy" â€” each has its place.

steps.title: ðŸ“ Mastering the Projects Feature
steps.content:
Projects is the organization tool that transforms Claude from a simple chat into a structured collaborative workspace. When you create a Project, you are creating a centralized space where you can gather conversations, documents, files, and knowledge related to a specific theme. Each Project has a context of 200,000 tokens â€” the equivalent of a 500-page book â€” allowing you to upload documents, code, brand guidelines, or any relevant information so Claude has a deep understanding of your work. Projects are available for Pro and Team users, and are ideal for teams working on long-term projects, product development, or strategic research. You can share snapshots of your best conversations with the whole team via an activity feed, inspiring colleagues and elevating the overall quality of collaborative work.

Practical tip: create Projects by type of work â€” one for your company's brand voice, another for technical documentation, another for strategic planning â€” that way when you switch context, all the information is organized and accessible.

steps.title: âœ¨ Artifacts: Creating Without Code
steps.content:
Artifacts are dedicated windows where Claude creates substantial, standalone content â€” code, websites, charts, documents, diagrams â€” all alongside your conversation. When you ask Claude to "create an interactive dashboard" or "make a responsive site", instead of everything appearing in the chat, the code appears in a separate window where you see the real-time preview. You can test it immediately, ask for adjustments conversationally ("make it more colorful", "add a button here"), and Claude iterates on the spot. Artifacts support React, HTML/CSS/JavaScript, SVG, Markdown, Mermaid diagrams, and much more. The best part? You can share these artifacts with anyone who has a Claude account, and when they use them, the token count goes against their subscription, not yours â€” eliminating API costs and deployment complexity.

Practical tip: use artifacts to create internal tools â€” custom calculators, data analyzers, template generators â€” and share them with your team without worrying about infrastructure or usage limits.

steps.title: ðŸ’» Code: Real-Time Execution
steps.content:
Claude doesn't just write code â€” it creates executable artifacts where you see the result working while you converse. If you ask for an interactive chart, it comes alive. A game? It's already playable. A calculator? Click and test. Execution happens immediately, without the need for a terminal, compilation, or setup. Claude can also integrate calls to your own API directly within artifacts â€” without exposed API keys, without additional costs per call, using your existing subscription. This means you can create functional AI-powered applications in minutes, not weeks. For debugging, simply describe the problem in natural language ("the calculator doesn't work with decimals") and Claude understands and fixes it. You can also "fork" â€” go back to any previous message, edit, and explore a completely different direction without losing your original work.

Practical tip: when something breaks, use "Fix with Claude" or simply describe the error as you would to a colleague â€” Claude translates natural feedback into code corrections automatically.

steps.title: ðŸ”— Claude's API: Power and Flexibility
steps.content:
Claude's API allows developers to integrate its intelligence into their own applications, chatbots, automated systems, or complex workflows. You access three main models: Claude Opus (most powerful, ideal for complex tasks), Claude Sonnet (best cost-benefit), and Claude Haiku (ultra-fast for simple queries). The API operates on a pay-per-token model â€” you only pay for what you use, for both input and output. It offers a context of up to 1 million tokens in some models, allowing the analysis of enormous documents. It supports function calling to integrate with your tools, web search for updated data, and image processing. Anthropic offers SDKs in Python, JavaScript/TypeScript, and other languages, easing integration.

Practical tip: if your application needs fast answers and low costs, start with Haiku; if it needs nuance and superior quality, invest in Opus. Test Sonnet first â€” it usually hits the perfect balance.

steps.title: ðŸ’ª Strengths of Claude's API
steps.content: Claude's API excels in tasks requiring deep analysis, quality writing, and complex reasoning. Its gigantic context (up to 1M tokens) allows processing entire documents, multiple PDFs, or long conversations in a single call â€” something that takes competitors much longer or costs more. Claude is particularly excellent at coding, structuring complex information, nuanced sentiment analysis, and generating content that requires voice consistency. The platform offers built-in ethical transparency â€” Claude warns when it is uncertain, refuses harmful tasks, and is honest about limitations. For teams, the integration with Projects and the ability to share artifacts without API costs transforms the way they work. The pricing model is predictable â€” you don't pay for failed requests, and shared use doesn't generate costs for the creator.

steps.title: âš ï¸ Weaknesses of Claude's API
steps.content:
Claude does not generate images â€” if your application needs to create visuals, you will have to integrate DALL-E, Midjourney, or another image tool. Its knowledge has a cutoff date (January 2025), so for real-time data, you need to combine it with web search or your own updated data sources. The API is more expensive per token than some competitors (ChatGPT offers lower prices on some models), although Claude delivers more quality, so it's a matter of trade-off. Rate limits can be restrictive if your application expects very high volume on the free tier. Claude also doesn't have access to a plugin ecosystem as vast as OpenAI's, so some specific integrations might require custom work. Finally, very new models might have higher latency while infrastructure scales â€” during demand peaks, answers might take longer than ChatGPT at certain times.

Practical tip: combine Claude with other tools â€” use Claude for analysis/writing, integrate third-party images, pull real-time data from your databases â€” this creates a more robust solution than trying to do everything with just one API.

steps.title: ðŸ• The History of DeepSeek
steps.content:
DeepSeek is an artificial intelligence developed by æ·±åº¦æ±‚ç´¢ (DeepSeek), a Chinese company focused on AI research. It gained global prominence for offering powerful models for free, with an immense context (up to 1 million tokens) and low development costs. When using DeepSeek, you see the AI's "train of thought", understanding the why behind the answer, not just the result.

DeepSeek is excellent at processing huge documents (entire books), complex programming, mathematics, and step-by-step logical reasoning. It's free, has a gigantic context, and is unbeatable for deep analysis of contracts, academic articles, and code debugging.

steps.title: âŒ When Not to Use DeepSeek
steps.content:
Do not use it for real-time information (news, weather, stock market) â€” it has no web browsing by default. It's also not ideal for "loose" creativity or very casual conversations, as it prioritizes precision and logic.

Practical tip: DeepSeek is for thinking, not for informing you about the present. For news, use web search.

steps.title: ðŸ¤– DeepSeek's Models
steps.content:
DeepSeek-V3: General-purpose model. Fast, great for conversations, writing, translation, and everyday tasks.

DeepSeek-R1: Pure reasoning model. Thinks before answering, showing chains of thought. Ideal for math, logic, and complex problems.

Practical tip: use R1 for difficult problems (code, math) and V3 for general tasks (emails, articles).

Be direct and specific. For R1, you don't need to say "think step by step" â€” it already does that. Clearly define the expected outcome and the format (list, JSON, etc). For V3, clear context and objective are sufficient.

Practical tip: "Solve this equation" (R1) or "Write a professional email about [subject]" (V3) work better than generic prompts.

steps.title: âš–ï¸ DeepSeek vs ChatGPT

ChatGPT is more versatile, has a plugin ecosystem, and integrates with other tools. DeepSeek is free, has a much larger context, and shows its reasoning. ChatGPT is better for creative tasks and web search; DeepSeek is unbeatable for deep analysis, long documents, and logic problems.

Practical tip: Use ChatGPT for "create 10 creative ideas". Use DeepSeek for "analyze this 300-page contract" or "explain this scientific theory in detail".

steps.title: ðŸ• The History of Gemini
steps.content:
Gemini is Google's multimodal artificial intelligence, launched in 2023 as a response to the advancement of models like GPT-4. It was built to process text, code, images, video, audio, and PDFs natively, without conversion between formats. In 2026, Google integrated Gemini across its entire ecosystem: Gmail, Docs, Drive, Slides, as well as specialized tools like Deep Research, NotebookLM, Imagen, and Veo. Gemini evolved from a simple chatbot into a complete AI platform integrated into the workflow.

Practical tip: Gemini is embedded in your Google Workspace â€” you might be using it without realizing it. Explicitly activating it generates insights about information you already have.

steps.title: âœ… Where Gemini is Very Good
steps.content:
Gemini excels in multimodal tasks â€” processing gigantic PDFs, analyzing long videos, or combining text with images is native. A context of up to 2 million tokens allows reading multiple books or entire repositories in one conversation. Deep Think surpasses competitors in scientific and mathematical reasoning. Native integration with Google Workspace offers contextual analysis impossible in standalone tools â€” Gemini understands your emails, documents, and patterns. Image generation via Imagen and videos via Veo are integrated. Deep Research gathers hundreds of web sources for deep market analysis.

Practical tip: use Gemini within Google Workspace â€” open the side panel in Docs or Drive and ask for an analysis of your documents. This is a superpower no other standalone assistant has natively.

steps.title: âŒ When Not to Use Gemini
steps.content:
Gemini is not ideal for maximum privacy â€” data processed on Google servers raises concerns for strict regulations (GDPR, HIPAA). For ultra-sensitive data, self-hosted Claude or DeepSeek are better. Without Deep Think, the technical reasoning is less specialized than Claude or DeepSeek. Gemini is proprietary â€” it does not offer transparency like open-weight DeepSeek. It's not the best for highly nuanced copywriting â€” Claude is more careful. Image/video generation can become expensive without control. The third-party plugin ecosystem is smaller than OpenAI's.

Practical tip: if privacy is critical, your team doesn't use Google Workspace, or you need total control, choose Claude or DeepSeek.

steps.title: ðŸ¤– Gemini's Models
steps.content:
Gemini 3 Pro is the flagship for AI Ultra subscribers, with 1 million tokens, ideal for complex problems. Gemini 3 Flash is the fast free standard for daily conversations. Gemini 3 Deep Think specializes in scientific and mathematical reasoning. Gemini 2.5 Pro and Flash are previous versions still available via API. Gemini Nano runs locally on smartphones. Imagen 3 generates hyperrealistic images. Veo generates videos. Code Assist helps with development.

Practical tip: for free use, start with Gemini 3 Flash. For heavy reasoning, upgrade to AI Pro or Ultra. For Deep Think, you need AI Ultra.

steps.title: ðŸ’¬ How Gemini Likes Its Users to Send Prompts

Gemini works best when you take advantage of its multimodal context â€” upload files instead of describing them. Be specific about the format: "Create a JSON with..." or "Summarize in 3 points". For Deep Think, you don't need to say "think deeply" â€” activate the button. Within Google Workspace, context is pre-loaded. For creative iterations, conversational feedback works well: "make it more vibrant" results in quick adjustments. Few-shot prompting (examples) improves quality.

Practical tip: simply upload your files (PDF, images, video). Don't describe â€” let the AI see it directly.

steps.title: âš–ï¸ Gemini vs Other AIs
steps.content:
Claude prioritizes safety and nuance. Gemini prioritizes integration and multimodality. DeepSeek offers maximum cost efficiency. ChatGPT is more universal. Gemini is better at analyzing gigantic documents and Google Workspace context. Claude is better for ethical analysis. DeepSeek excels in pure mathematics. ChatGPT is best for starting universally. Gemini offers native image and video generation. The choice is: Google integration (Gemini), safety (Claude), efficiency (DeepSeek), or versatility (ChatGPT)?

Practical tip: use Gemini in Google Workspace, Claude for deep analysis, DeepSeek for math, ChatGPT for universality.

The API is offered via Google AI Studio (free with limits), direct Gemini API (pay-per-token), and Vertex AI (enterprise). It supports up to 2 million tokens in some models. Automates comprehensive research â€” you ask a question and Gemini gathers hundreds of sources, synthesizes, and generates a report with cited references. Connects related themes automatically. Includes citations â€” use directly in assignments. Transforms "Google + Wikipedia" research into an integrated analysis with reliable sources.

Practical tip: use Deep Research as a starting point for research â€” saves hours and offers an integrated view of the topic.

steps.title: âœ… Complete Workflow
steps.content:
(1) Gather Material â€” NotebookLM centralizes PDFs and classes.
(2) Research â€” Deep Research explores a new topic.
(3) Brainstorm â€” Gemini Live for exploratory ideas.
(4) Structure â€” Google Docs + Gemini panel for outlining and expansion.

steps.title: âš ï¸ Important Precautions
steps.content:
Gemini still hallucinates in unstructured tasks â€” review for critical information. It is an assistant, it doesn't replace critical thinking. Always verify the citations offered. Sensitive data is on Google servers. Audio Overview doesn't replace full reading. Code Assist offers suggestions â€” it's not always the best solution. Deep Research might include questionable sources â€” validate them. Always maintain critical judgment.

Practical tip: use Gemini to accelerate work, but always validate critical information with the primary source.

steps.title: ðŸ“š THE HISTORY OF COPILOT
steps.content:
Copilot was born as a vision from Microsoft in 2023: to integrate AI directly into the products that billions of people already use every day.

Billions of people use Windows, Word, Excel, and Outlook daily. When Microsoft placed Copilot "inside" these apps, it wasn't a new app to learn â€“ it was an evolution of what already existed.

Companies of all sizes started using it: from SMBs using basic Microsoft 365 to large corporations integrating Copilot into their workflows.

Unlike other chatbots, Copilot doesn't require you to open a new tab. It's right there, integrated, waiting to be used.

Marketing professionals, HR, sales, developers, and data analysts use Copilot to save hours on repetitive tasks.

ðŸ’¡ PRACTICAL TIP: If you already use Microsoft 365, you already have Copilot. No need to subscribe to anything new to get started. Look for the blue star icon in your apps.

steps.title: âš™ï¸ HOW COPILOT WORKS
steps.content:
Copilot is an artificial intelligence trained to understand and generate text, but with a difference: it accesses the specific context of your computer.

When you open a document in Word and ask "Summarize this text", Copilot isn't just generating a generic response. It is analyzing:
- The keywords of your open document
- The language pattern used
- The work context (whether it's a corporate email, a report, a contract)

It works using mathematical patterns to recognize which word should come after another. It's not "consciousness" or "true comprehension" â€“ it's extremely sophisticated probabilistic calculation.

The better you describe what you need, the better the result. "Summarize" is vague. "Summarize in 3 bullet points the main risks mentioned" is precise.

ðŸ’¡ PRACTICAL TIP: Always give context to Copilot. Instead of "Analyze this", say "Analyze this Q3 sales report and identify which regions grew above 15%". Specificity = better answers.

steps.title: âœ¨ WHERE COPILOT IS VERY GOOD
steps.content:
Copilot is excellent in tasks that involve your Microsoft ecosystem:

- In Excel: Analyze data, create charts, identify trends, clean messy data. Example: "Copilot, which products had a drop in sales last month?" â€“ it analyzes your open spreadsheet and answers.
- In Word: Summarize long documents, generate ideas for what's missing, review tone, expand short sections. Example: "Expand the methodology section maintaining academic formality".
- In PowerPoint: Generate slides from a brief, redesign layouts, add content based on a theme. Example: "Create 5 slides about quarterly results with this data".
- In Outlook: Summarize long email threads, generate context-based replies, prioritize important messages.
- In Windows: Help with settings, search for files, execute system tasks.
- In code: If you use Visual Studio, Copilot suggests code based on your project's context.

ðŸ’¡ PRACTICAL TIP: Copilot shines in tasks "inside your file". If you have an Excel spreadsheet open, Copilot understands the data context and can do much more accurate analyses than if you just pasted the data into a generic chat.

steps.title: âŒ WHERE COPILOT IS VERY BAD
steps.content:
There are clear limits to Copilot:

- Real-time research: Copilot does not have access to current information by default. If you ask "What is the price of Bitcoin right now?", it doesn't know.
- Original creativity: If you need something completely new that doesn't follow known patterns, Copilot might get stuck on generic answers.
- Deep critical analysis: For critical business decisions, Copilot might sound confident but be completely wrong.
- Sensitive information: If your file has confidential data, remember that Copilot processes this. It's not always safe to use on ultra-sensitive documents.
- Outside the Microsoft ecosystem: If your main work is in Google Workspace, Figma, or other platforms, Copilot won't help much.
- Fact validation: Copilot can invent data convincingly. If you ask "List 5 studies that prove X", Copilot might invent titles and authors that look real.

ðŸ’¡ PRACTICAL TIP: Never use Copilot's answer as the "final word" on something critical. Always validate. For sensitive data, carefully configure your Microsoft account regarding which files Copilot can access.

steps.title: âš ï¸ ATTENTION - IMPORTANT CHARACTERISTICS
steps.content:
**Copilot Works By Patterns, Not Comprehension**
When Copilot "reads" your document, it is not truly understanding it like a human being. It is recognizing statistical patterns and predicting the next words with extremely high precision.

If you ask "Summarize the 3 main criticisms of this report", Copilot identifies:
- Sections that use critical language
- Negative keywords
- Structural patterns of criticism

But it doesn't really "comprehend" the frustration or emotional importance. It's math, not consciousness.

**Hallucination: Copilot Can Invent**
Just like other AI models, Copilot hallucinates â€“ it invents data to appear useful.
Real scenario: "Copilot, which clients increased spending in 2024?" If Copilot can't find the information clearly, it might generate numbers that look real but were invented.
Why does it happen? Copilot's goal is to be convincing and useful, not necessarily 100% truthful.

**Context is Strength and Weakness**
Strength: Copilot has access to your open file. This allows much more relevant analyses.
Weakness: If the file has incomplete or incorrect data, Copilot will amplify the error. Garbage in, garbage out.

ðŸ’¡ PRACTICAL TIP: Before using Copilot on critical data, check if your source data is clean. If the spreadsheet has 500 rows with errors, Copilot will generate analyses based on bad data.

steps.title: âœï¸ THE ART OF THE PROMPT â€” FROM BASIC TO ADVANCED
steps.content:
Most people only use 20% of Copilot's potential because they write weak prompts. Look at the evolution:

**Level 1** â€” Weak prompt: "Summarize this document."

**Level 2** â€” Medium prompt: "Summarize this report in 5 bullet points."

**Level 3** â€” Strong prompt: "Summarize this sales report in 5 bullet points, highlighting the regions with a drop above 10%, using executive language to present to the board."

**Level 4** â€” Professional prompt: "You are a senior business analyst. Analyze this Q3 sales report, identify the 3 largest regional drops, suggest hypotheses for each drop, and format the answer in bullet points for a 10-minute executive presentation."

The difference between level 1 and level 4 is huge. Copilot is the same. What changes is who knows how to ask.

steps.title: ðŸ¢ USE CASES BY AREA (HR and Sales)
steps.content:
ðŸ‘¥ **Human Resources:** Create customized job descriptions by level and area. Summarize resumes and highlight key points. Draft internal communications with the appropriate tone. Generate interview scripts based on the job profile. Create structured onboarding plans.

ðŸ“Š **Sales:** Analyze pipeline spreadsheet and identify at-risk deals. Generate personalized follow-up emails by client. Summarize interaction history of a client before a meeting. Create approach scripts for different buyer profiles. Analyze goals vs. actuals and suggest corrective actions.

steps.title: ðŸ¢ USE CASES BY AREA (Marketing, Management, and Tech)
steps.content:
ðŸ“£ **Marketing:** Generate copy variations for email campaigns. Summarize customer feedback and identify patterns. Create content briefs from performance data. Adapt the tone of a text for different audiences. Generate monthly content plans based on industry trends.

ðŸ“‹ **Management and Leadership:** Summarize meeting minutes and list next steps with assignees. Create executive presentations from raw data. Draft structured feedback for the team. Prioritize task list by impact and urgency. Generate project status reports in clear language.

ðŸ’» **Technology and Data:** Explain code snippets in simple language. Suggest improvements to existing scripts. Generate technical documentation from code. Analyze logs and identify error patterns. Create test cases based on requirements.

steps.title: âš ï¸ MOST COMMON ERRORS AND HOW TO AVOID THEM
steps.content:
**Error 1 â€” Trusting numbers blindly:** Copilot can generate statistics that look real but were invented. Always validate numerical data in reliable sources before presenting to anyone.

**Error 2 â€” Prompt too vague:** Asking "improve this text" without stating the goal, tone, and audience generates generic results. Be specific about what to improve and for whom.

**Error 3 â€” Not reviewing the output:** Copilot is a starting point, not a final product. All generated content needs human review before being used professionally.

steps.title: âš ï¸ MORE COMMON ERRORS
steps.content:
**Error 4 â€” Using it on messy data:** If the spreadsheet has inconsistencies, empty cells, or duplicated data, Copilot will generate wrong analyses with high confidence. Clean the data before analyzing.

**Error 5 â€” Ignoring the context of the open file:** Many people open Copilot and type the question without having the relevant file open. Copilot's power lies in analyzing what is on your screen. Always work with the file open.

**Error 6 â€” Giving up on the first bad result:** If the first result wasn't good, refine the prompt. Add more context, specify the desired format, or ask it to try again with a different approach.

steps.title: ðŸ” ADVANCED PROMPT TECHNIQUES (Part 1)
steps.content:
**Role Technique:** Assign a role to Copilot before asking the question. "You are an HR expert with 20 years of experience. Analyze this resume and say if the candidate is suitable for the commercial manager position." This completely changes the quality of the answer.

**Constraint Technique:** Limit what Copilot can do to force precision. "Answer in a maximum of 3 sentences. Do not use technical jargon. Use language that a non-technical manager can understand."

**Example Technique:** Show the format you want before asking. "I want an answer in this format: Problem: / Cause: / Solution: / Next step: Now analyze this sales situation and answer in this format."

steps.title: ðŸ” ADVANCED PROMPT TECHNIQUES (Part 2)
steps.content:
**Iteration Technique:** Use Copilot in rounds. First ask for a draft version, then ask to improve specific parts, then ask to adjust the tone. Build the result in layers.

**Reviewer Technique:** After generating content, ask Copilot itself to criticize what it generated. "Now analyze this answer you gave and tell me which points might be wrong or incomplete." This increases the reliability of the output.

steps.title: ðŸ“± COPILOT IN DAILY LIFE â€” PRACTICAL WORKFLOW
steps.content:
**Morning:** Open Outlook and ask Copilot to summarize priority unread emails. Ask it to suggest quick replies for those needing a response.

**During meetings:** Use Copilot in Teams to transcribe and summarize the meeting in real-time, listing decisions made and next steps with assignees.

**When working on documents:** Before writing from scratch, ask Copilot for a suggested structure. Then fill it in with your knowledge and ask for specific refinements.

**When closing the day:** Ask Copilot to review what was produced and list pending tasks for the next day based on the open documents.

steps.title: âš¡ THE HISTORY OF GROK
steps.content:
GROK was born in 2023 as a creation of xAI, the AI company founded by Elon Musk. Its proposal was different: an AI with real-time access to the internet and X (formerly Twitter) conversations.

While other AIs had knowledge "frozen" on specific dates, GROK was developed to be constantly updated. Its name is a reference to the book "The Hitchhiker's Guide to the Galaxy" â€“ and its personality reflects that: irreverent, with a sense of humor, and willing to talk about topics other AIs avoid.

xAI's strategy was clear: not to compete by being the same as the others, but by being different. GROK prioritizes access to real data in real-time and a less "filtered" approach than competitors. This made it popular among users seeking quick answers about news, trends, and current events.

ðŸ’¡ PRACTICAL TIP: GROK is available on X (formerly Twitter) for premium users. If you already pay for X Premium, GROK can be your real-time research assistant without needing to leave the social network.

steps.title: ðŸ§  HOW GROK WORKS
steps.content:
GROK is an artificial intelligence trained to understand and generate text, but with a major differentiator: constant access to the internet and X data.

When you ask a question, GROK doesn't just consult its pre-trained knowledge (which has a cutoff date). It accesses:
- Trending topics on X in real-time
- Current web news
- Public conversations and social media data
- Continuously updated information

This means if you ask "What is happening in the cryptocurrency market right now?", GROK can consult today's data, not data from months ago.

It works using language patterns combined with real-time search. It is not "consciousness" or "real opinion" â€“ it is pattern analysis in continuously updated data.

GROK was also trained with the philosophy of being "maximally truthful and minimally censurable", which means it tries to be honest about limits and avoids generic refusals when possible.

ðŸ’¡ PRACTICAL TIP: Use GROK when you need updated information about events, trends, or news. For timeless tasks (history, concepts, math), other AIs might be equally good, but GROK wins on recent data.

steps.title: ðŸ”¥ IT IS ONE OF THE AI TRENDS OF THE MOMENT
steps.content:
GROK grew quickly because it is integrated into X, a platform with hundreds of millions of monthly users.

There is no need to download a new app or open a separate browser tab. For those who already use X Premium, GROK is right there, accessible with one click.

Journalists use GROK to verify news in real-time. Investors use it to monitor markets. Content creators use it to research trends. Researchers use it to access updated data.

The competitive advantage is clear: access to real-time data that other AIs don't have. If ChatGPT knows data from April 2024, GROK knows from today.

Companies and professionals are also starting to integrate GROK into their workflows because the speed and recency are unmatched.

ðŸ’¡ PRACTICAL TIP: If you follow financial markets, politics, or social trends, GROK is practically mandatory. The advantage of always being up to date is huge for these areas.

steps.title: ðŸ’ª WHERE GROK IS VERY GOOD
steps.content:
GROK is excellent in tasks requiring updated information or real-time context:

- Researching news and events: "What is happening with the dollar exchange rate right now?" GROK accesses updated data and answers.
- Trend analysis: "What are the most discussed topics on X today?" GROK sees real-time trends.
- Market monitoring: Stock prices, cryptocurrencies, commodities â€“ GROK has access to current data.
- Recent fact-checking: "Did this candidate really say that yesterday?" GROK can consult today's speeches and news.
- Social research: "What is the sentiment of people about this topic?" GROK analyzes X and web conversations.
- Content about current affairs: If you need to write about an event that happened today, GROK provides updated data.
- Decisions dependent on current data: Are you thinking about buying a stock? GROK shows recent performance and current news.
- X and social media analysis: Because GROK is integrated into X, it understands conversation contexts and platform trends better than anyone else.

ðŸ’¡ PRACTICAL TIP: When you have a question starting with "now", "today", "recently", use GROK. Its differentiator is exactly being up to date in real-time.

steps.title: â›” WHERE GROK IS VERY BAD
steps.content:
There are clear limits to GROK, despite its advantages:

- Deep analysis of timeless concepts: If you want to understand the history of philosophy or quantum physics concepts, GROK is no better than other models.
- Tasks requiring personal context: GROK does not access your files, private documents, or personal data. For analyzing your own business, other AIs coupled with your systems are better.
- Creativity without parameters: If you need completely original fiction, GROK might be less creative than alternatives.
- Extreme privacy: GROK works within X, which collects data. If you want maximum privacy, it is not the best option.
- Reliability in specific numbers: Despite accessing real data, GROK can still make calculation errors or misinterpret data.
- File context: Unlike Copilot or Claude, GROK doesn't analyze your private documents. It can't read a contract you uploaded.
- Polarization: Because GROK was trained with data from X, which is a polarized social network, its answers sometimes reflect biases present on the platform.
- Reverse censorship: In its attempt to be "minimally censurable", GROK can sometimes offer content that crosses lines of common sense or sensitivity.

ðŸ’¡ PRACTICAL TIP: Do not use GROK for tasks requiring maximum privacy or analysis of your internal data. Use it for what it is good at: real-time intelligence based on the web and X.

steps.title: âš ï¸ ATTENTION - IMPORTANT CHARACTERISTICS
steps.content:
**GROK Accesses Real-Time Data, But Can Still Be Wrong**
GROK's great advantage is having updated data. But "updated" does not mean "perfect". GROK can still:
- Interpret news incorrectly;
- Confuse correlation with causality;
- Cite sources that seem reliable but are wrong;
- Make calculation errors with real numbers;
Real-time is an advantage, not a guarantee of accuracy.

**GROK has Personality, Not Neutrality**
Unlike other AIs that try to be "neutral", GROK was developed with a personality: irreverent, humorous, willing to question consensus.
This is good because it makes the experience more human. But it also means GROK has tendencies and biases reflected in its answers. It is not "objectively neutral".
If you ask about a politically sensitive topic, GROK might take a more defined stance than other AIs.

**Integration with X Is Strength and Weakness**
Strength: GROK understands the context of X better than any AI. If you want analysis of trends, people's sentiment, or what's happening on the network, GROK is superior.
Weakness: GROK is limited to the X platform. If the subject does not have a significant presence on X, GROK might be at a disadvantage.

**GROK Can Also Hallucinate**
Despite access to real data, GROK still hallucinates. It can:
- Invent citations that look real
- Create dates that correspond to no events
- Generate numbers that look like real data but were fabricated
Web access helps, but doesn't eliminate the problem completely.

ðŸ’¡ PRACTICAL TIP: Always check GROK's critical information against at least 2 sources. Just because GROK has access to real-time data doesn't mean every answer is 100% accurate. Use it as a starting point, not as absolute truth.

steps.title: âš ï¸ MOST COMMON ERRORS AND HOW TO AVOID THEM (Part 1)
steps.content:
**Error 1 â€” Treating Grok as absolute truth:** Real-time doesn't mean perfect. Grok might misinterpret news or cite inaccurate sources. Always validate critical information across at least two sources before taking action.

**Error 2 â€” Using Grok for timeless tasks:** To understand philosophy, math, history, or scientific concepts, other AIs are equally good or better. Save Grok for what makes it unique: current data.

steps.title: âš ï¸ MOST COMMON ERRORS AND HOW TO AVOID THEM (Part 2)
steps.content:

**Error 5 â€” Not leveraging X integration:** Many people use Grok as a generic chatbot and forget that it understands X's context better than any other AI. Questions about trends, public sentiment, and conversation analysis are where it truly shines.

**Error 6 â€” Not questioning hallucinations disguised as real data:** Web access gives responses more credibility, but Grok can still invent quotes, dates, or numbers that look real. Be skeptical of highly specific data and verify.

steps.title: ðŸ” ADVANCED PROMPT TECHNIQUES
steps.content:
**Timeframe Technique:** Always include the timeframe in the question. "In the last 24 hours", "this week", "today", or "this month" direct Grok to use its real differentiator instead of answering with generic knowledge.

**Sentiment Technique:** Ask Grok to analyze how people are reacting to a topic, not just what is happening. "What is the general sentiment on X about this news?" is a question only Grok answers well.

**Temporal Comparison Technique:** Ask for comparisons between past and present. "How does the price of Bitcoin today compare to 30 days ago, and what events explain this difference?" This generates rich analyses using the real-time advantage.

**Alert Technique:** Use Grok as a monitor. "Warn me about the main tech market movements today and highlight any red flags that deserve attention." This turns Grok into an on-call analyst.

**Verification Technique:** Before publishing or sharing information, use Grok to verify. "Is this statement true based on today's data? What sources confirm or contradict it?" This reduces the risk of spreading misinformation.

**Morning:** Open Grok on X and ask for a summary of the main events in your industry from the last 12 hours. This replaces reading several news portals in minutes.

**Before important meetings:** Quickly search what is being said about the meeting's topic on X and the web. Arriving updated with today's data makes a real difference.

**When creating content:** Before posting about any topic, check what's trending and the public sentiment on that topic at that specific moment.

**When making decisions with market data:** Use Grok to get a quick overview of what's happening before making any decision that relies on updated information.

**When monitoring your brand or competitors:** Ask Grok what is being said about specific names on X today. It's real-time reputation monitoring without needing paid tools.

steps.title: ðŸ” HISTORY OF PERPLEXITY
steps.content:
Perplexity AI was created in 2022 as an answer to the question: "What if internet search was reimagined with AI?"

While Google offered links, Perplexity offered direct answers. The concept was revolutionary: instead of clicking 10 links to find the answer, you ask and receive a synthesized response with cited sources.

Founded by Aravind Srinivas and his team, Perplexity positioned itself as "the search engine of the future". It grew rapidly because it solved a real problem: people don't want links, they want answers.

Unlike GROK (integrated into X) or Copilot (integrated into Microsoft 365), Perplexity is independent and exclusively focused on research. Its differentiator is being the best at finding real-time information and presenting it clearly with citations.

In 2024, Perplexity was already processing billions of searches and became a real threat to Google in certain user segments.

ðŸ’¡ PRACTICAL TIP: Perplexity is free to use on the web. Go to perplexity.ai, ask a question, and see how it works. If you like it, there's a Pro version with more features and a more powerful model.

steps.title: ðŸ§© HOW PERPLEXITY WORKS
steps.content:
Perplexity is an AI-based search engine that combines real-time search with synthesized answers.



When you ask a question on Perplexity:
- **Real-time search:** It accesses the internet and finds the most relevant pages for your question.
- **AI Synthesis:** Instead of returning links, it uses artificial intelligence to synthesize a coherent answer.
- **Source citation:** Every piece of information has a numbered citation showing exactly where it came from.

For example, if you ask "What is Brazil's current inflation?", Perplexity doesn't just answer "8.2%", but also:
- Explains the context
- Cites exactly which source stated it
- Offers links for you to investigate further if you wish

It works using language patterns combined with truth-seeking. It's not "genuine thought" â€“ it's pattern analysis on real, updated data.

Perplexity uses different AI models internally (Claude, GPT-4, or its own model), depending on which version you use.

ðŸ’¡ PRACTICAL TIP: Use Perplexity when you want an answer with cited sources. Traditional search (Google) is better for casual exploration. Perplexity is better when you want a precise answer with evidence.

steps.title: âš¡ IT IS ONE OF THE MOST USED AIs
steps.content:
Perplexity grew exponentially because it offers something Google doesn't do well: direct answers synthesizing multiple sources.

Users discovered that Perplexity is faster than clicking 5 Google links. Researchers use it because it saves time. Journalists use it to fact-check quickly. Students use it because it understands context.

Unlike ChatGPT (which has frozen knowledge) or GROK (which is a social network), Perplexity is purely focused on research. It is not tied to any platform.

The company raised millions in funding and now competes directly with Google in search. Some users have already completely migrated from Google to Perplexity for productivity.

Professionals who need to do a lot of research (lawyers, analysts, journalists, consultants) consider Perplexity essential.

ðŸ’¡ PRACTICAL TIP: If you do a lot of searches during the day, try Perplexity Pro for a month. The difference in speed and answer quality can quickly offset the cost in productivity.

steps.title: âœ… WHERE PERPLEXITY IS VERY GOOD
steps.content:
Perplexity is excellent in tasks that require quick research with reliable sources:

- **Searching for news and current events:** "What happened in the tech market this week?" Perplexity aggregates news and synthesizes it into an answer.
- **Fact-checking:** "Is this statement true?" Perplexity searches multiple sources and cross-references information.
- **Academic and professional research:** "What is the current state of research on generative AI?" Perplexity synthesizes recent papers and articles.
- **Comparative analysis:** "What is the difference between Perplexity, ChatGPT, and GROK?" Perplexity compares and cites sources for each point.
- **Long context search:** If you ask a complex question with context, Perplexity understands it better than a simple Google search.
- **Purchasing decisions:** "Which smartphone is best in 2024?" Perplexity aggregates up-to-date reviews, prices, and specs.
- **Scientific research:** "What are the latest advances in nuclear fusion?" Perplexity accesses recent papers and scientific news.
- **Learning:** If you want to understand a new concept, Perplexity synthesizes explanations from multiple sources into a coherent answer.

ðŸ’¡ PRACTICAL TIP: When you ask a question on Perplexity, click the numbered citations to see exactly where the info came from. This builds trust and allows independent validation.

steps.title: âŒ WHERE PERPLEXITY IS VERY BAD
steps.content:
There are clear limits to Perplexity, despite its advantages:

- **Original creativity:** If you want fiction, a script, or original creative content, Perplexity is not the tool. It searches the internet â€“ it doesn't invent.
- **Tasks requiring private context:** If you need to analyze your own documents, Perplexity won't work (it has no access to your files).
- **Complex calculations:** Despite searching for info, Perplexity might not be the best for intricate math problems.
- **File and spreadsheet analysis:** Unlike Copilot, Perplexity cannot open and analyze your Excel, Word, or PDF files.
- **Very obscure or local topics:** If you are looking for something very specific with little online presence, Perplexity will struggle.
- **Extreme privacy:** Since Perplexity searches the internet, your searches leave traces. If you want maximum privacy, it's not ideal.
- **Deep critical analysis:** For critical business decisions, Perplexity offers synthesis, not deep analysis like Claude would.
- **Prolonged conversation:** Perplexity is better for standalone questions. For long, iterative conversations, other models are better.
- **Persistent hallucination:** Despite citing sources, Perplexity can still hallucinate if the info doesn't exist or is too new.

ðŸ’¡ PRACTICAL TIP: Use Perplexity for "What?" and "How much?" (objective facts with sources). Use Claude or ChatGPT for "How?" and "Why?" (analysis and creative synthesis).

steps.title: âš ï¸ ATTENTION - IMPORTANT CHARACTERISTICS
steps.content:
**Perplexity Synthesizes, It Doesn't Create**
Perplexity's great strength is that it searches for real info and synthesizes it. But this is also a limitation: it cannot invent or go beyond what exists on the internet.
If info is too new (less than a few hours), Perplexity might not have access yet. If it's very old, there might be conflict between different versions.
Perplexity doesn't "understand" â€“ it recognizes patterns across billions of web pages and groups the best answers.

**Citations Are Good, But Not Always Perfect**
One of Perplexity's biggest differentiators is that it cites sources. This is excellent. But:
- Cited sources might be wrong or outdated
- A source might be misinterpreted
- Multiple sources might be wrong about the same topic
Citation is not a guarantee of truth. It is just traceability.

steps.title: âš ï¸ SEARCH AND MODEL CAUTIONS
steps.content:
**Real-Time Search Has Limits**
Perplexity accesses the internet, but it does not consult EVERY page. It consults the pages its algorithms consider most relevant. This means:
- Information you might search for on Google might not appear on Perplexity
- Biases in search algorithms affect the answers
- Some sources are prioritized, others ignored

**Internal Model Affects Quality**
Perplexity offers different versions (free uses a simpler model, Pro uses Claude or GPT-4). The answer quality depends on which model is running underneath. A weak model + great search = mediocre answer.

ðŸ’¡ PRACTICAL TIP: If you use free Perplexity and the answer doesn't seem good, try the Pro version. The difference in the internal model is significant.

steps.title: âš ï¸ MOST COMMON ERRORS AND HOW TO AVOID THEM (Part 1)
steps.content:
**Error 1 â€” Using Perplexity for creative tasks:** It synthesizes what exists on the internet, it doesn't create original content. To write a script, a creative campaign, or fiction, use Claude or ChatGPT.

**Error 2 â€” Trusting citations without verifying:** Citation is not a guarantee of truth. The source could be outdated, misinterpreted, or simply wrong. For critical decisions, click on the citations and read the original source.

**Error 3 â€” Asking about very recent subjects:** If an event happened a few hours ago, Perplexity might not have access yet. For breaking news, Grok has the upper hand by being integrated into X in real-time.

**Error 6 â€” Asking questions that are too vague:** Perplexity performs best with specific and well-defined questions. The vaguer the question, the more generic and less useful the generated synthesis will be.

steps.title: ðŸ” ADVANCED PROMPT TECHNIQUES (Part 1)

**Deep Dive Technique:** After receiving an answer, use the citations as a starting point to go deeper. Click on the cited source, read the original, and return to Perplexity with a more specific question based on what you read.

steps.title: ðŸ¤– HISTORY OF MANUS
steps.content:
Manus was launched in March 2025 by the Chinese startup Butterfly Effect. The name comes from "Mens et Manus" (mind and hands in Latin), perfectly reflecting its premise: an AI that not only thinks but executes actions in the digital world just like a human colleague would.

When launched, Manus caused an explosive impact â€“ it was compared to the DeepSeek phenomenon for its revolutionary ambition. It started in a closed beta phase with a waitlist and restricted access. The differentiator was clear: while ChatGPT generates text and Copilot analyzes files, Manus automates complete tasks from start to finish.

The biggest change came recently: Manus completely removed the waitlist system and opened registration to everyone.

steps.title: âš™ï¸ HOW MANUS WORKS
steps.content:
Manus is an autonomous agent that goes far beyond any chatbot. It doesn't just generate answers â€“ it executes complete tasks as if it were a human professional working on your computer.

When you make a request to Manus, it works in layers:
1. **Interpretation:** You describe the goal. Manus understands not just the words, but the complete context of the task.
2. **Planning:** Manus divides the task into sequential sub-tasks and defines the correct order.
3. **Autonomous Execution:** This is where Manus sets itself apart. It doesn't just recommend â€“ it does. It opens a browser, searches in real-time, collects data, writes code, executes commands.
4. **Total Transparency:** You see everything happening on a "Virtual Computer". The AI types, clicks, navigates, makes decisions. You follow along in real-time and can stop it if it's wrong.
5. **Delivery:** The result is concrete. It's not a suggestion â€“ it's a finished artifact. Published website, functional code, structured analysis, operational automation.

Internally, Manus uses advanced models like Claude 4.6 Sonnet (Anthropic) and customized versions of the Qwen family (Alibaba).

steps.title: ðŸ’¡ Practical Usage Tip
steps.content: The more specific the prompt, the better. "Create a site" is vague. "Create an e-commerce site with 'electronics' category products, with a functional cart, search, price filters, and reviews" is perfect. Manus understands and executes exactly that.

steps.title: ðŸ“Š MARKET IMPACT AND ADOPTION
steps.content:
Manus has grown almost vertically since opening public access. The removal of the waitlist was transformational â€“ in days, tens of thousands of users gained access.

**Who uses it:** Developers needing rapid prototyping. Designers wanting mockups in minutes. Data analysts automating weekly reports. Marketers doing competitive research in hours. Journalists compiling data from multiple sources. Consultants structuring strategies. Entire startups using Manus to execute tasks that would take an entire team weeks.

**Measured impact:** In rigorous tests like GAIA, Manus outperformed OpenAI's Deep Research. It's not "experimentation" â€“ it's real competitive performance.

Companies report significant savings: a task that took 8 hours of human labor takes 30 minutes with Manus (and with review, 1 hour). Repetitive tasks that took weeks now take days. Prototyping that took weeks now takes hours.

ðŸ’¡ PRACTICAL TIP: Follow @ManusAI_HQ on X for updates. New features appear monthly. Discord communities share advanced prompts and techniques that save credits. Investing 30 minutes to learn techniques can save hundreds of credits later.

- **Generation of complete web pages:** "Turn this document into a professional website" â€“ Manus generates fully functional HTML/CSS/JavaScript with responsive design.
- **Web application development:** "Create an analytics dashboard" â€“ Manus structures the logic, generates interfaces, implements charts and filters.
- **Competitive research automation:** "Analyze 15 SaaS competitors, prices, features, integrations" â€“ Manus navigates sites, collects data, creates a structured comparative matrix.
- **Automatic report generation:** "Compile Q3 sales, create charts, identify trends, write insights" â€“ All done in minutes, not hours.
- **Dataset analysis:** "Process 50,000 customer records, identify patterns, generate segmentation" â€“ Manus executes analyses that would take days manually.
- **Prototype creation:** "Prototype a delivery app with professional UI/UX" â€“ Structured code, modular components, ready for development.

steps.title: ðŸ’ª WHERE MANUS IS VERY GOOD (Part 2)
steps.content:
- **Mass data organization:** "300 emails into categories, summarize each, create spreadsheet with insights" â€“ Complete autonomous execution.
- **Data extraction and transformation:** "Extract info from these PDFs, organize in Excel, cross with sales data" â€“ Multiple steps, Manus executes sequentially.
- **Structured content generation:** "Research 2025 AI trends, create 5 video scripts with structure, keywords, timings" â€“ Research + synthesis + structuring.
- **Landing page development:** "Create landing page for AI webinar" â€“ Design, copywriting, optimized CTA, all functional.
- **Data integration from multiple sources:** "Combine API, CSV, and web scraping data into a single dashboard" â€“ Orchestration of multiple tasks.
- **Visual UX/UI prototyping:** "Create wireframes and mockups for a productivity app" â€“ Visual structure ready for refined design.

ðŸ’¡ PRACTICAL TIP: Manus is best for tasks that would take 10+ hours manually. If it's something that takes 30 minutes, other AIs are sufficient. For real savings, use Manus for projects that have multiple complex steps.

steps.title: âŒ WHERE MANUS IS VERY BAD
steps.content:
There are clear limits:

- **Genuine creativity:** If you want original fiction, creative storytelling, or completely new content, Manus is limited. It researches and synthesizes known patterns, it doesn't invent unique stories.
- **Critical decisions without validation:** Health, law, personal finance â€“ Manus can make severe mistakes. It always needs expert human validation.
- **Extreme confidentiality:** Everything goes through the Manus platform. Ultra-sensitive data, critical IP â€“ you run a risk.
- **Data hallucination:** Despite executing in real-time, Manus can still invent numbers, cite wrong sources, or misinterpret data.
- **Private file context:** Manus does not access your local files directly. It works in the web/online environment.
- **Tasks requiring continuous human decision:** If it needs "creative choice" at every step, Manus takes over and might guess wrong.
- **Escalating cost:** Complex tasks consume many credits quickly. A large automation might consume 1,000+ credits.
- **Inconsistent quality:** Sometimes it executes perfectly, sometimes it makes unexpected choices on the exact same type of task.

steps.title: ðŸŽ¯ HOW TO EXTRACT GOOD RESULTS FROM MANUS
steps.content:
Most bad results come from vague prompts. Manus responds to precision. Use this formula:

**[CONTEXT] + [CLEAR TASK] + [PATTERN/STYLE] + [CONSTRAINTS] + [EXAMPLE]**

âŒ **Bad example:** "Create a site"
âœ… **Good example:** "Create a digital marketing agency website. It must have: home, portfolio with 10 projects, about, services (4 main ones), blog with 5 recent articles, contact form. Style: modern, minimalist, blue and white colors. Mobile responsive. With basic SEO implemented."

**Divide Giant Tasks into Steps:**
If your task is "Create a complete e-learning platform", Manus might get confused or spend 1,000+ credits. Divide:
- Step 1: Create the HTML/CSS structure of 5 pages (home, courses, dashboard, player, profile)
- Step 2: Implement JavaScript for basic interactivity
- Step 3: Create API mock for dynamic data
- Step 4: Add basic authentication

steps.title: âš ï¸ MOST COMMON ERRORS AND HOW TO AVOID THEM (Part 1)
steps.content:
**Error 1 â€” Vague prompt in a complex task:** Asking to "create a complete platform" without specifying pages, features, style, and constraints is the fastest way to burn credits and get a bad result. Always use the complete formula.

**Error 2 â€” Not monitoring the Virtual Computer:** Letting Manus run unwatched is risky. If it makes a wrong decision early on, it will build everything on top of it. Monitor execution and stop early if something goes off track.



**Error 3 â€” Trying to do everything in a single giant task:** Large projects must be broken into steps. An e-learning platform isn't one task, it's 4 or 5 sequential steps. This saves credits and maintains consistent quality.

steps.title: âš ï¸ MOST COMMON ERRORS AND HOW TO AVOID THEM (Part 2)
steps.content:
**Error 4 â€” Trusting critical data without validating:** Manus can hallucinate numbers, cite wrong sources, or misinterpret data even while searching in real-time. Financial, medical, or legal data always need specialized human validation.

**Error 5 â€” Using Manus for simple tasks:** If the task takes 30 minutes manually or with another AI, Manus is not the right choice. It shines in 10-hour+ tasks. For simple things, you are wasting expensive credits.

**Error 6 â€” Ignoring the community and updates:** Manus launches new features monthly. Techniques that worked 3 months ago may have improved. Joining Discord communities and following the official profile saves credits and amplifies results.

steps.title: ðŸ” ADVANCED PROMPT TECHNIQUES (Part 1)
steps.content:
**Complete Formula Technique:** Always structure prompts with the five elements: context, task, pattern, constraints, and example. Each element eliminates a decision Manus would have to make alone, and autonomous decisions can go wrong.

**Step Division Technique:** Large projects should become smaller sequential projects. Clearly define where each step begins and ends before starting. This maintains control and allows correcting before building upon an error.



**Checkpoint Technique:** At each partial delivery, review before requesting the next step. "Is what's delivered so far correct? Yes, continue. No, correct this before moving forward." This prevents cascading rework.

steps.title: ðŸ” ADVANCED PROMPT TECHNIQUES (Part 2)
steps.content:
**Visual Reference Technique:** Mention sites, products, or brands that serve as inspiration for the expected style. Manus can research and adapt real references, which greatly elevates the visual quality of the result.

**Iterative Feedback Technique:** Don't expect perfection on the first delivery. Receive, review, and ask for specific adjustments on concrete parts. "The home page looks great. Just adjust the services section to have 4 cards with icon, title, and short description." This refines without redoing everything.

steps.title: ðŸ“± MANUS IN DAILY LIFE â€” PRACTICAL WORKFLOW
steps.content:
**Before starting any task:** Write the complete prompt with the formula before sending. Two minutes planning the prompt saves 30 minutes of rework and hundreds of credits.

**During execution:** Keep the Virtual Computer visible and monitor in real-time. If something goes off track, interrupt early. Every wrong step Manus takes costs credits and generates rework.

**Upon receiving delivery:** Review the delivery carefully before approving and advancing. Check data, links, functionality, and visuals. Only request the next step when the current one is validated.

**For long projects:** Document completed and pending steps. Treat Manus as a professional executing a project, with partial deliveries and clear approvals at each phase.
